concepts.lesson: During a chemical reaction, the total mass of the products is always equal to the total mass of the reactants. This means that mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction. When substances react to form new substances, the atoms are simply rearranged.
concepts.real_example: If 5.3 g of sodium carbonate reacts with 6 g of ethanoic acid, the total mass of the products (carbon dioxide, water, and sodium ethanoate) will be exactly 11.3 g.
key_points: Mass of reactants = Mass of products

concepts.lesson: In a chemical substance, the elements are always present in definite proportions by mass. This is also known as the Law of Definite Proportions. No matter where the sample comes from or how it is prepared, the ratio of the masses of its constituent elements remains the same.
concepts.real_example: In pure water, the ratio of the mass of hydrogen to the mass of oxygen is always 1:8. If 9 g of water is decomposed, 1 g of hydrogen and 8 g of oxygen are always obtained.
key_points: Independent of the source of the compound

concepts.lesson: John Dalton provided an explanation for the laws of chemical combination. He proposed that all matter is made up of very tiny particles called atoms, which are indivisible and indestructible. Atoms of a given element are identical in mass and chemical properties.
concepts.real_example: Dalton used different symbols for elements, like a circle with a dot for Hydrogen, to represent these indivisible building blocks.
key_points: Atoms of different elements have different masses
Atoms combine in small whole number ratios

concepts.concept_name: Atomic Mass and Relative Atomic Mass
concepts.lesson: Since atoms are extremely small, their absolute mass is difficult to measure. Instead, we use relative atomic mass. It is defined as the average mass of the atom as compared to 1/12th the mass of one carbon-12 atom. One atomic mass unit (u) is a mass unit equal to exactly one-twelfth the mass of one atom of carbon-12.
concepts.real_example: The atomic mass of Oxygen is 16 u, which means an oxygen atom is 16 times heavier than 1/12th of a carbon-12 atom.

concepts.lesson: A molecule is the smallest particle of an element or a compound that is capable of an independent existence. Molecules of an element are constituted by the same type of atoms (e.g., $O_2$), while molecules of compounds are formed by atoms of different elements joining together in definite proportions (e.g., $H_2O$).
concepts.real_example: Argon ($Ar$) is monoatomic, while Oxygen ($O_2$) is diatomic.
key_points: Atomicity = number of atoms in a molecule

concepts.lesson: Compounds composed of metals and non-metals contain charged species known as ions. A negatively charged ion is an anion, and a positively charged ion is a cation. Ions can consist of a single charged atom or a group of atoms (polyatomic ions) that have a net charge.
key_points: Polyatomic ions carry a fixed charge

concepts.lesson: The chemical formula of a compound is a symbolic representation of its composition. To write it, we use the symbols of the constituent elements and their valencies (combining power). We use the 'criss-cross' method where the valencies of the two elements are exchanged to become subscripts.
concepts.real_example: For Magnesium Chloride, Magnesium has valency 2 and Chlorine has 1. Criss-crossing gives $MgCl_2$.
key_points: Metal is written first in the formula

concepts.concept_name: Molecular Mass and Formula Unit Mass
concepts.lesson: Molecular mass is the sum of the atomic masses of all the atoms in a molecule of a substance. Formula unit mass is used for substances whose constituent particles are ions. Both are calculated by adding the atomic masses of all atoms present in the formula.
key_points: Formula unit mass is for ionic compounds
